I think this is going to throw an exception if error_on_empty is False.  The pre-ceding code will throw an expection if error_on_empty is True, but if error_on_empty is False, `mon_initial_members` could be None.  If it is, this is going to call re.split(...., None), and that will lead to a TypeError.  You can't give None to split()

Instead, this needs to be:

```
if not mon_initial_members:
    if error_on_empty:
         raise...
else:
    mon_initial_members = re.split(r'[,\s]+', mon_initial_members)
return mon_initial_members
```

Please use `env=None`. Default values are bound at method definition time, not at evaluation time.

```
>>> def foo(x={}):
...     x[random.randint(0, 10)] = 1
...     print(x)
... 
>>> foo()
{4: 1}
>>> foo()
{4: 1, 0: 1}
>>> foo()
{4: 1, 0: 1, 7: 1}
```

Python 3 style is for iterators to have a special method `__next__` and then invoke the built-in function `next()` to call it.

The `next()` method is retained as an alias only for Python 2 compatibility -- in Python 3, the iterator's next method is not meant to be invoked directly.

Okay, we have a problem here, I only realized it today.

If we do two consecutive restarts, the current_block_number will change, and the behavior of the node **could** change *event though it should not*, and the only solution for this seems to be the block number from the event.

It required that all channels we have would be counter-deposited, but this may not be the case when we connected to a client already saturated. This will just assert our side of our channels were funded.

So there are 2 questions here.

One is the UI side, which I think we all agree should show the deposit amount you are about to deposit and not the total deposit we will have after deposit call succeeds.

On the API side having an idempotent call is a big plus. So I agree with Augusto that on our API side having a deposit of total amount function call makes sense. So something like `set_total_deposit()`.

> Does it make sense to have both topUp(added_amount) and setDeposit(total_amount)

I would say it does not make sense to have both.

Also the one problem I can see with this idempotency is that you will need to approve the contract to transfer tokens for you. So there you are going to have to use the actual amount of tokens about to top up and not the total.
